players.name: Lawrence Ati-Zigi
players.bio: Ghana's first-choice shot-stopper, Ati-Zigi has been a rock between the posts for St. Gallen and the Black Stars. Commanding in the air and sharp with his feet, the 29-year-old brings calm authority to Ghana's backline.
players.facts: His surname is sometimes mistaken for a Kansas city — Lawrence, Kansas has zero World Cup caps.

players.name: Alidu Seidu
players.bio: Rapid and tenacious, Alidu Seidu is one of the most exciting full-backs in Ligue 1. The Rennes man uses his blistering pace to snuff out attackers and bomb forward, making him a dual threat on Ghana's right flank.
players.facts: Born in 2000, Seidu is part of a golden Ghanaian generation — and he already has an international goal to his name.

players.name: Caleb Yirenkyi
players.bio: A teenager with serious ambition, Yirenkyi has impressed in Denmark's Superliga with Nordsjaelland — a club famous for developing African talent. At just 20, this World Cup could be his global coming-out party.
players.facts: Born in January 2006, Yirenkyi was only 4 years old when Ghana famously reached the 2010 World Cup quarter-finals.

players.name: Jonas Adjetey
players.bio: Accra-born and Wolfsburg-bred, Adjetey is a composed young centre-back who reads the game well beyond his years. At 22, he's already earning Bundesliga minutes and brings fresh defensive steel to Ghana's 2026 squad.
players.facts: At 22, Adjetey plays in the Bundesliga for Wolfsburg — one of only a handful of Ghanaians in Germany's top flight.

players.name: Thomas Partey
players.bio: The heartbeat of Ghana's midfield for over a decade, Partey's thunderous passing range and tenacious pressing made him a Premier League favourite. Now at Villarreal, the veteran leads by example with 57 caps and 15 goals.
players.facts: Partey's father Yakubu also played professional football — making them one of Ghana's most famous footballing families.

players.name: Abdul Mumin
players.bio: A rugged and reliable centre-back, Abdul Mumin has carved out a solid career in La Liga with Rayo Vallecano. Despite limited caps, the Accra-born defender brings top-flight Spanish experience to Ghana's defence.
players.facts: Mumin has spent most of his career in Spain, making him one of a select group of Ghanaians thriving in La Liga.

players.name: Abdul Fatawu
players.bio: Electrifying, direct, and impossible to ignore, Abdul Fatawu is one of West Africa's brightest young talents. The Tamale-born winger has dazzled at Leicester City and arrives at his first World Cup aged just 22.
players.facts: Born in 2004, Fatawu made his senior Ghana debut as a teenager — younger than most players get their first club contract.

players.name: Kwasi Sibo
players.bio: A box-to-box midfielder with a solid defensive instinct, Kwasi Sibo has built his career steadily through European football. The Wa-born midfield enforcer brings grit and industry to Ghana's engine room.
players.facts: Sibo hails from Wa in Ghana's Upper West Region — one of the most northerly cities in the country, close to Burkina Faso.

players.name: Jordan Ayew
players.bio: Son of the legendary Abedi Pele, Jordan Ayew carries Ghanaian football royalty in his blood. With 120 caps and 34 goals, the veteran is one of the Black Stars' greatest servants and a natural leader at his fourth World Cup.
players.kit: Nike Mercurial Superfly VII Elite FG
players.facts: Jordan's father Abedi Pele won African Player of the Year three times. No pressure at the dinner table, then.

players.name: Brandon Thomas-Asante
players.bio: Born in Milton Keynes and raised on English football, Brandon Thomas-Asante is a powerful, energetic striker who never stops running. He chose Ghana over England and brings a never-say-die attitude to the Black Stars' attack.
players.facts: Thomas-Asante was born in Milton Keynes — a town perhaps better known for its roundabouts than its football heroes.

players.name: Antoine Semenyo
players.bio: London-born but proudly Ghanaian, Semenyo is a pacey, skilful winger who earned his move to Manchester City through relentless hard work. His direct running and eye for goal make him one of Ghana's most dangerous weapons.
players.facts: Semenyo was born in London but swapped England's grey skies for Ghana's Black Stars — and hasn't looked back since.

players.name: Joseph Anang
players.bio: A reliable backup between the posts, Joseph Anang honed his craft in English football before settling at St Patrick's Athletic in Ireland. The Teshie-born keeper is ready to answer the call if duty comes knocking.
players.facts: Anang plays his club football in the League of Ireland — making him one of the most unique World Cup keepers in 2026.

players.name: Christopher Bonsu Baah
players.bio: A tricky, fast-footed forward, Christopher Bonsu Baah has taken his talents to Saudi Arabia's Pro League with Al-Qadsiah. Still just 21, the attacker is brimming with potential and ready to shine on the biggest stage.
players.facts: Bonsu Baah is one of several young Ghanaians playing in the Saudi Pro League — a surprisingly well-trodden path.

players.name: Gideon Mensah
players.bio: An overlapping left-back with energy to burn, Gideon Mensah has been a consistent presence for Ghana across 40 caps. The Auxerre defender combines defensive discipline with an appetite to get forward and create.
players.facts: With 40 caps, Mensah is one of Ghana's most experienced defenders — yet he's still only 27 years old.

players.name: Elisha Owusu
players.bio: Born in France but firmly committed to Ghana, Elisha Owusu is a tenacious midfielder who wins the ball and keeps things ticking. His Ligue 2 experience at Auxerre has sharpened him into a dependable midfield option.
players.facts: Owusu was born in Montreuil, France, just outside Paris — making him one of several Franco-Ghanaians in this squad.

players.name: Benjamin Asare
players.bio: A veteran presence in the goalkeeping ranks, Benjamin Asare has been a stalwart for Hearts of Oak — one of Ghana's most storied clubs. At 33, the third-choice keeper brings experience and composure to the squad.
players.facts: Asare plays for Hearts of Oak, one of the oldest and most successful clubs in Ghanaian football history, founded in 1911.

players.name: Abdul Rahman Baba
players.bio: A marauding left-back known for his pace and powerful frame, Baba Rahman has graced some of Europe's biggest clubs including Chelsea. Now at PAOK, he brings seasoned European pedigree to Ghana's defensive ranks.
players.facts: Baba Rahman joined Chelsea in 2015 for around £22m — quite the price tag for a lad from Tamale, Ghana.

players.name: Jerome Opoku
players.bio: London-born and technically polished, Jerome Opoku is a centre-back who has found his footing in Turkish football with Başakşehir. Composed under pressure, the 27-year-old is a reliable option across Ghana's back line.
players.facts: Opoku was born in London and came through the lower leagues of English football before making it internationally.

players.name: Iñaki Williams
players.bio: Born in Bilbao and raised in Spain, Iñaki Williams is one of football's great loyalty stories — spending his entire career at Athletic Bilbao. He switched allegiance from Spain to Ghana in 2022 and hasn't looked back.
players.facts: Iñaki holds the record for most consecutive La Liga appearances. His brother Nico also plays — but chose Spain instead.

players.name: Augustine Boakye
players.bio: A lively and creative midfielder from Bompata, Boakye has been turning heads in Ligue 1 with Saint-Étienne. Yet to earn a senior cap, the 25-year-old arrives at the World Cup with a point to prove — and plenty to offer.
players.facts: Augustine Boakye is uncapped at senior level — making a potential World Cup debut all the more extraordinary.

players.name: Kojo Peprah Oppong
players.bio: Young, ambitious, and technically gifted, Kojo Peprah Oppong is a promising defender who has caught the eye at Nice. The Accra-born 22-year-old is one of the fresher faces in Ghana's 2026 World Cup squad.
players.facts: At just 22, Oppong plays in Ligue 1 for Nice — a club that has become a pipeline for emerging African defensive talent.

players.name: Kamaldeen Sulemana
players.bio: Quick, skilful, and utterly unpredictable, Kamaldeen Sulemana is the kind of winger defenders dread meeting. The Atalanta flyer from Techiman has dazzled in Serie A and brings explosive flair to Ghana's left flank.
players.facts: Kamaldeen's dribbling stats regularly rank among the best in Serie A — defenders across Europe know his tricks all too well.

players.name: Derrick Luckassen
players.bio: Amsterdam-born Luckassen is a physically imposing centre-back who chose Ghana over the Netherlands. After stints across Europe, he's landed at Pafos in Cyprus and brings a well-travelled defensive intelligence to the squad.
players.facts: Luckassen is Dutch-born and was previously linked with the Netherlands setup before committing to the Black Stars.

players.name: Ernest Nuamah
players.bio: A pacy and direct winger from Kumasi, Ernest Nuamah has lit up Ligue 1 with Lyon and earned admirers across Europe. With 4 goals in 18 caps, the 22-year-old is becoming one of Ghana's most potent attacking threats.
players.facts: Nuamah joined Lyon from Nordsjaelland — the Danish academy that has become a famous finishing school for African talent.

players.name: Prince Kwabena Adu
players.bio: Hailing from the small town of Sampa in Ghana's Bono Region, Prince Kwabena Adu has punched well above his postcode to reach Czech football with Viktoria Plzeň. A raw but exciting attacker with a big future ahead.
players.facts: Adu hails from Sampa in the Bono Region — a tiny Ghanaian town that can now boast a World Cup squad member.

players.name: Marvin Senaya
players.bio: Born in Saint-Maurice, France, Marvin Senaya is a versatile defender who has committed his international future to Ghana. With Auxerre experience behind him, he's a composed and dependable squad option for the Black Stars.
players.facts: Senaya is one of three Auxerre players in this Ghana squad — the French club has quietly become a Black Stars hotspot.
